data.title: DigitalBridge: An Acquisition Could Be A Good Deal For SoftBank And Investors Alike
data.description:
Introduction

DigitalBridge Group, Inc. (DBRG) has been in the news recently due to rumors that a company heavily invested in the AI growth story is looking to take it private to help with its grand project. I wanted to go through the story in more detail and give some comments on the whole situation.

If you are already invested, I would hold off from buying or selling right now, as the rumors are getting quite interesting with many good price points that the acquirer could offer. I wouldn’t gamble on it if I didn't already have any skin in the game, mainly because I am already heavily weighted in AI plays.

Potential Acquisition

Just a few days ago, the company’s downward momentum on its share price was broken due to a potential acquisition by SoftBank Group Corp. (SFTBY). Bloomberg reported that the two companies are in advanced talks, with the aim of taking DBRG private, amid surging AI-driven demand for digital infrastructure, especially growth sectors like data centers. It is still in very early talks, as neither of the companies is willing to comment on the situation, but apparently, according to sources close to the matter, the deal might come through at the end of the year, which is just a few weeks from now. On what seems like a rumor, DBRG’s stock price rocketed 46% on around 56m shares traded, which is around 10 times the average volume.

A Bit about SoftBank

SoftBank’s CEO has been very prominent in the investing community. Masayoshi San pivoted the company aggressively to AI sometime back in 2023, after its Vision Fund posted losses for a whole year straight. The two funds suffered substantial losses of over $80B due to many bets on unprofitable startups during the low-interest rate euphoria back even before COVID-19 took the world by storm. Companies like WeWork, which was its biggest investment failure, had to be written down by over $10B. SoftBank was all in on such startups right before the pandemic. WeWork, the Uber IPO (UBER), and DiDi (DIDIY), all suffered dramatic losses due to the pandemic, forcing Masayoshi to take the loss and pivot. It seems that the man just got very unlucky; nobody could have predicted a pandemic.

Since then, the company has pivoted hard into AI, with massive bets on Nvidia (NVDA), although the company did sell its full early stake for a profit of around $3.3B back in 2019; however, it has missed $150B since then, as the share price went from around $35 to $140. Finally, after many years in the red, the company turned a profit in FY24.

SoftBank is very heavily involved and could say all in on the new growth story, which is undeniably the main talk of the Street, and that is AI and data centers.

Why SoftBank wants DBRG?

The rationale behind this move is rather simple. DBRG is operating in a very hot sector right now. The hottest sector since the dotcom bubble and fiber optics. And actually, the company is still involved heavily in cell towers and fiber optics to accelerate AI infrastructure. By acquiring DigitalBridge, SoftBank essentially gains instant access to its 20.0GW power capacity and the many established data centers, like Vantage’s $25B Texas Campus, massive fiber network, and $108B AUM portfolio. This way, SoftBank will be able to bypass many years of regulatory hurdles, grid connection, and unnecessary costs to build it all from scratch. That would have taken years, literally, and it seems a lot riskier than just buying the company.

Synergies

Since the company is all in now, there are a lot of synergies from buying DBRG. Masayoshi San and the company launched a massive project back in January of this year, in partnership with OpenAI (OPENAI), Oracle (ORCL), and MGX, called Stargate, which targets $500B investment in U.S. AI infrastructure. Masayoshi San is at the helm of the project and is aiming to build data centers in Texas for OpenAI. This is a massive project that is going to span at least four years. Here lies the first synergy point. I mentioned DBRG has a stake in Vantage’s data center, Texas Campus, which comes with a 1.4GW pipeline. Why does that matter? The Stargate Project saw some delays in the area due to insufficient capacity to support the energy requirements; therefore, DBRG’s hyperscaler contracts and fiber networks will enable GPU deployments at the Stargate sites, like the one in Abilene, TX, which is its main location. As DBRG has a 20.9GW pipeline, it provides the project with an instant bank of power. Without the company, that would have most likely meant a 3-5 year waiting time to achieve such scale.

The unprecedented scale that DBRG garnered through years of infrastructure investments in exactly what SoftBank is looking for is very appealing and much more efficient than building from scratch. What kind of costs are we talking about here? Well, apparently, the Stargate Project requires 7GW of power and around $400b of investment, so DBRG’s investment in Vantage’s data center Texas Campus 1.4GW pipeline comes at a massive discount and could cover 20% of the total power needs of the project, for a fraction of the price.

Many analysts came out with their price ranges for how much the company could acquire DBRG for. JPMorgan said it could be sold for as little as $25 a share, and that is quoted as a conservative price, with up to $35 a share upside. That is still a massive premium even after the rumors began circulating, as the company is trading at around $15 a share now. Wells Fargo sees at least $18-$20 a share, which is a lot more conservative, while RBC Capital raised its target price to $23 a share. So, let’s say we take $30 a share as the buy price; that’s double the DBRG’s market cap, bringing it to around $5B. $5B for access to 1.4GW of power is much better than around $57B ($400B investment for 7GW).

This is going to benefit SoftBank, OpenAI, and Oracle tremendously, as it could mean bypassing grid delays that have been plaguing the project since the beginning.

It seems like a no-brainer acquisition.

Briefly on DBRG’s Recent Performance

The company reported fee-related earnings of around $93m, which translates to around 22% y/y growth, so still very healthy. One odd thing we can see on the company’s financials is a massive, carried interest fee reversal of around $120m, which brought down its GAAP revenue this quarter to just under $4m. Carried interest is based on the performance of the funds above specific hurdle rates, which are in the range of 6%-8%, so if the fund doesn’t go above the range of returns, the company has to give back some of that accrued interest, which in this case amounted to $120m.

How you should look at this is to essentially ignore it and focus on fee-related earnings. The company’s Fee-Earning Equity Under Management or FEEUM reached $40.7B, up 19%, and earnings were up 43%, which is very impressive. Carried interest, or performance fee, is nice, but it is not always guaranteed. What is always guaranteed is the 1%-2% fee on FEEUM. So, the company’s core operations are growing and very healthy.

Risks and Closing Comments

The deal is far from done. None of the parties confirmed this rumor as of yet, which means it is pure speculation for now. Only the people close to the two companies have any idea what is happening or what is not happening. What should you do? I think if you have already owned the company for a while during this quite underwhelming performance over the last 5 years (down 22%), I don’t see a reason not to hold on for now to see where this drama leads, if anywhere.

The company’s portfolio is fantastic, focusing on the main growth stories in the modern era, and big tech is increasing its AI-related capex spend dramatically in 2026, which means there is a lot of money to go around, so just follow the money.

I am not going to be buying right now since the rumor is not fully confirmed, which means if it does not come to life, I expect to see a correction once again. If that does happen, I would consider the company a lot more attractive; however, I am quite heavily weighted into the AI growth story, so even then, I would probably avoid it.

But if you don’t have enough exposure to AI somehow, I would say DigitalBridge Group would be a good company to invest in even if it doesn’t go private and the deal doesn’t happen.

data.title: Capital One: What's (New) In Your Wallet
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the RGA Investment Advisors Q3 2025 Investment Commentary.

We recently added shares of Capital One (COF) to our portfolios. At one point, through our shares in ING (ING) years ago, we owned COF indirectly. This resulted from ING's divestiture of ING Direct to Capital One, in exchange for cash and shares totaling just shy of 10% of the company. This acquisition gave Capital One a low-cost, digitally native (branchless) deposit base with a state-of-the-art user interface and consumer experience. Though we never owned COF directly, we watched it admirably for years. When COF announced their Discover (DFS) acquisition, we thought it was too good to be true and feared regulators could prevent the tie-up from happening, but we did our work as we watched to see what would happen. As luck would have it, regulators eventually approved the deal and we now own COF directly.

The acquisition of DFS is a transformative transaction that uniquely positions COF to generate substantial value, fundamentally redefine its competitive standing, and reshape the US payments landscape. By integrating DFS's proprietary network and customer base, COF is shifting from being primarily a card issuer to a vertically integrated payments entity, akin to an American Express (AXP)-like operation with scale rivaling the largest payments networks in the world.

The most immediate and transformative value driver of the acquisition is the ownership of the Discover (DFS) network, which allows COF to avoid regulatory constraints that cap interchange fees for most large banks. This network capability is a "game-changer" that provides an immediate boost to fee revenues by repapering and issuing debit cards to all existing COF customers.

Specifically, the acquisition allows COF to leverage a key exemption related to the Durbin Amendment, which typically caps debit interchange fees, by issuing debit cards on its owned network and negotiating rates directly with merchants. Experts estimate that this switch will result in a pickup of approximately 75 basis points (BPS) in debit interchange rates, leading to an implied weighted average rate of 1.25% on a $50 transaction, compared to the existing Durbin-capped rate of 0.49%. This ability to monetize its massive debit volumes is projected to deliver over $1.0 billion in network synergies by 2027. COF will invest considerably in its merchant network, especially abroad, so that its customer value can truly rival what the Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA) (MA) networks offer their own debit customers. As it currently stands, COF is well prepared to do so.

We think it is less likely, though possible that COF eventually pursues this strategy on the credit side of things. Even if they do not go all-in, as they are in debit, there are levers the company can pull to capture greater economics in the credit card network. As it stands, this acquisition elevates COF's scale, making the combined entity the largest credit card issuer in the country and the second largest by transaction volume. Even if COF keeps the credit card setup as is, owning a network and added degrees of scale provides real leverage in future renewal negotiations with Visa and Mastercard.

Central to realizing this value is Capital One's sophisticated technological infrastructure and operational expertise. COF has historically made substantial investments in its tech stack, designed specifically to grow through acquisition and convert new portfolios efficiently onto its platform. This technological playbook is crucial for integrating Discover (DFS)'s systems, especially on the loan and asset management side, where integration is a core competency for COF. This integration capacity drives confidence in achieving significant operational benefits. COF has guided toward realizing approximately $1.5 billion in annual expense savings within two years, representing about 23% of Discover's operating expenses.

Redefining Capital One and Enhancing Its Banking Franchise

The merger facilitates COF's redefinition by broadening its consumer base and strengthening its banking foundation. Discover's portfolio includes prime and student credit customers, complementing COF's traditional strength in the "main street, new to credit" and subprime markets. This shift improves the pro forma loan book mix by directing it toward less consumer subprime risk. The combination with Discover's lower-loss loan book is expected to act as a dampener in future regulatory stress tests. Furthermore, the consolidation eliminates a key top competitor in the main street credit card segment, reducing new customer acquisition costs.

On the banking front, the combined institution would rise from 9th to 6th among US banks by deposits. Discover's own historical strength in online savings combines nicely with COF's online bank that emerged from the ING Direct acquisition over a decade ago.

In preparation for the merger, both COF and DFS accumulated large excess capital balances, resulting in substantial capacity for capital returns to shareholders. To that end, COF recently increased its dividend by 33% and announced a new $16 billion share repurchase program. We expect at least $10b in repurchase activity (or over 7.5% of market cap at today's valuation) each of the next two years, with capacity to either continue repurchases at an aggressive rate or to grow the dividend, depending on how cheap or expensive shares trade.

The translation of these strategic advantages and capital return into shareholder value makes COF an attractive investment opportunity today. The DFS acquisition is more than a simple consolidation; it is a strategic metamorphosis. Taken together, this strategic vertical integration transforms COF into a major payments player and the company is now one of the most important domestic entities in this key space within the financial sector.

The traditional driver of value for banks is tethered to the company's balance sheet and its ability to profitably underwrite loans. Banks are heavily regulated and as such, it is challenging to earn meaningfully in excess of bank cost of capital, effectively constraining the ability to earn very high ROEs in the space. Some banks, including COF have excelled at earning ROEs consistently above their cost of capital, but few have earned ROEs above 20%. A thriving payments franchise with its return profile driven by network effects, technology and intangible assets that do not exist on a balance sheet can earn ROEs well above 20% and now Capital One owns a scaled, rapidly growing piece of this market.

This convergence of network economics and operational efficiency acts like a flywheel, spinning off immense cash flow that can be continuously recycled to investors through accelerated capital returns, while growth in net income accelerates. We expect the combined entity to earn $25 or more in EPS come 2027, versus 2024's $14 in EPS. Most importantly, we expect the bank's Return on Tangible Common Equity (ROTCE) to exceed 20% by 2027, justifying a meaningfully higher multiple in the market than today's 12x trailing and 10x forward P/E.1 Look at a group of comparable P/Es on this year's earnings:

Ticker P/E FY 2025
BAC 13.65
C 13.14
WFC 13.32
GS 16.11
MS 16.35
AXP 22.32
JPM 15.02
COF 10.35
— —
Peer Average 15.03
Average Excluding COF 15.7
ROE Advantaged Average 17.45

The average peer trades right around a 15x P/E. The ROE advantaged group that have earnings streams untethered to the balance sheet trade at nearly 17.5x. At $25 in 2027 EPS, the stock is simply mispriced: even a pedestrian 15x multiple implies ~70% upside, and a hybrid bank / payment-network multiple implies a double.

Jason Gilbert, CPA/PFS, CFF, CGMA, Managing Partner, President

Elliot Turner, CFA, Managing Partner, CIO

Footnotes

1 Pinegap - AI Platform for Equity Research
